你所做的任何改变，无论大小，即使是在Gitup之外，都会立即反映在Gitup的图表中。没有提神，没有等待。
Gitup让您完全、透明地控制您的本地结账，因此很容易从不需要的更改中退出。
玛格丽塔酒、纹身、Sudo RM-RF/…。生活中有些事情是无法挽回的。但是，Gitup可以像cmd-z一样轻松地撤销您最近的更改。
Gitup的快照功能为回购所做的每一次更改构建了一个类似时间机器的历史记录，让您可以倒退到任何时间点。
重写、拆分、删除和重新排序提交、修复和挤压、挑樱桃、合并、重新设置基地-所有这些都在这里，而且速度非常快。
Gitup把力量放在你的指尖上。冲浪您的回购，进行更改，并通过几个短键将其全部倒回。
Gitup在不到一秒的时间内加载并呈现官方Git回购的40,000个提交的整个图表。
按消息、作者甚至不同的内容在repo中搜索分支、标签和每次提交，然后立即跳转到结果。
因为它绕过了Git二进制工具，直接与repo数据库交互，所以Gitup比其他Git客户机可靠得多，而且通常比命令行更快。
Gitup的Live Map和整洁的UI为Git新手提供了理想的学习环境，而Undo&amp；Snapshot功能可以让您安全地学习和体验。
note_en:
Any change you make, large or small, even outside GitUp, is immediately reflected in GitUp's graph. No refreshing, no waiting.
  GitUp gives you full, transparent control over your local checkout, so it's easy to back out from unwanted changes.
  Margaritas, tattoos,  sudo rm -rf / …some things in life can't be undone.  But GitUp makes undoing your latest changes as easy as cmd-z.
  GitUp's Snapshot feature builds a Time-Machine-like history of every change made to your repo, allowing you to step backwards to any point in time.
  Rewrite, split, delete, and re-order commits, fixup and squash, cherry-pick, merge, rebase—It's all here, and it's lightning-fast.
  GitUp puts the power in your fingertips.  Surf your repo, make changes, and rewind it all back with a few short keystrokes.
  GitUp loads and renders the entire graph of 40,000 commits for the official Git repo in less than a second.
  Search branches, tags and every commit in the repo by message, author and even diff content, then jump to the results instantly.
  Because it bypasses the Git binary tool and interacts directly with the repo database, GitUp is vastly more reliable than other Git clients and often faster than the command line.
  GitUp's Live Map and clutter-free UI make an ideal learning environment for Git newcomers, while the Undo & Snapshots features let you learn and experiment safely.
title: Gitup--你一生都怀念的Git界面
title_en: GitUp – The Git interface you've been missing all your life

note:
华尔街日报报道：领导力他们认为公司的性别多样性对于确保技术不会惩罚代表不足的人至关重要。
2009年，当拉娜·埃尔·卡略比(Rana El Kaliouby)向投资者推销她的人工智能初创公司Affectiva时，她和她的联合创始人试图避开她所说的“e-word”--情感。
她们都是女性，虽然她们的初创公司旨在探测科技中的情感，但她们对自己会被如何看待很敏感。他们担心自己可能不会被认真对待，因为“情感”并不在许多公司和资助者的传统词汇中--也因为在一个以男性为主的行业中，它蕴含着女性的含义。
“我们围着它跳舞，”她说，并补充说，他们称自己是一家“情绪化”公司。“投资者投资于他们所知道的，我们与他们习惯的情况大不相同.”
十年后，这种情况正在改变。尽管人工智能领域仍然严重由男性主导，但女性领导人已经取得了明显的进展。一些人表示，他们希望建立对不同劳动力更具吸引力的工作场所，并帮助更广泛地提升科技行业的女性。其他人表示，他们希望努力解决人们对这项技术的担忧，根除他们认为这项技术对边缘化社区的潜在偏见。
“技术不会很好地发挥作用，除非它是由一个多元化的团队建立起来的，”埃尔·卡略比博士说。她补充说，硅谷缺乏性别多样性，这与她在埃及上大学时看到的计算机科学学生的情况形成了鲜明对比，在埃及，性别划分几乎是平均的。“我今天在人工智能领域最大的担忧不是机器人会接手，而是我们无意中在这些系统中建立了偏见。”
El Kaliouby博士现在对她的公司的描述中接受了情感这个词，在担任公司首席技术官多年后，她于2016年成为公司的首席执行官。
最近，解决人工智能偏见的需求得到了鲜明的体现，Twitter表示，它需要对其图片裁剪算法进行更多分析，因为用户发现，Twitter有时似乎更喜欢在推文预览中显示白人面孔，而不是黑色面孔。该公司表示，在之前的测试中没有发现种族或性别偏见的证据。
根据PitchBook和All Raise的一份报告，2018年，涉及至少有一名女性创始人的公司的风险投资交易有3477笔，高于2009年的583笔。All Raise是一家寻求推动女性进入科技行业的非营利组织。该组织发现，虽然65%的美国大型风投公司仍然没有单一的女性合伙人，但去年有创纪录的52名女性成为合伙人，高于前年的38名。
鉴于人工智能要求人们努力解决数据中的公平性和代表性问题，Textio的首席执行官兼联合创始人基兰·斯奈德(Kieran Snyder)表示，女性创始人和思想领袖在该领域特别引人注目也就不足为奇了。Textio使用人工智能来分析职位描述中的语言。
在创办自己的公司之前，斯奈德曾在微软工作，她回忆说，有一次，她的经理说，她必须等到产假结束后才能获得晋升机会，届时他将能够更好地衡量她是否仍然忠于自己的工作。(微软没有回复记者的置评请求。)。
她说：“我认为，寡不敌众的人更有可能意识到为什么准确、公平的数据集很重要，并想要做这项工作。”她补充说，女性可能因此更容易被这一领域吸引。
麦肯锡公司(McKinsey&Amp；Co.)首席多样性和包容性官拉雷娜·易(Lareina Yee)说，从很多方面来说，科技行业女性不足是美国最近才出现的现象。她也是麦肯锡全球科技业务的领导者之一。例如，根据美国国家科学基金会(National Science Foundation)的数据，1995年，女性占美国所有学习计算机科学的本科生的29%，到20年后，这一数字下降到了18%。
在整个硅谷，尽管女性担任技术职位的比例在某些情况下有所上升，但仍然很低：例如，2019年，谷歌美国技术职位中女性占24%，高于2014年的17%。
易女士说，一个挑战是，硅谷的文化希望成功人士看起来像马克·扎克伯格(Mark Zuckerberg)，而疏远了那些不符合这种模式的人。例如，来自编程女孩(Girls Who Code)和埃森哲(Accenture)的一份新报告发现，在科技行业工作的女性中，有一半在35岁之前退出了这个行业，文化问题是首要原因。
作为一名女性首席执行官，使用人工智能帮助识别新药的Verge Genology的联合创始人爱丽丝·张(Alice Zhang)表示，她为公司设定了不同的基调。她说：“有很多领导风格都是用拳头拍打桌子--‘我们做得太好了，我们正在粉碎它，我们正在扼杀它，’”她说。“我的想法更多地基于情感上的脆弱和诚实。”
全球咨询公司Willis Towers Watson本月与彭博(Bloomberg LP)合作进行的一项研究发现，领导层性别更平衡的公司的员工往往表现出更高的敬业度，更有可能留在雇主那里。
在创立她的公司Node之前，法隆·法特米是谷歌的早期员工之一，她说，在谷歌，她很幸运地得到了斯泰西·布朗-菲尔波特(Stacy Brown-Philpot)等女性的指导。斯泰西·布朗-菲尔波特是她的第一位经理，直到最近才担任TaskRabbit的首席执行官。法特米说，一旦她创立了Node--该公司销售一个帮助企业构建和部署人工智能模型的软件平台--她就深思熟虑地试图创造一个对女性友好的工作场所，在早期提供丰厚的生育福利，并与同事坦率地谈论自己冷冻卵子的经历。
作为一名女性，法特米说，某些事情，比如向全是男性的投资者推销，要困难得多。但其他人来得更容易。
“我们从第一天起就一直是多元化的--作为一名女性创始人，你会吸引这一点，”她的公司的法特米说。她的公司大约有40%的女性员工，包括产品开发和领导团队。
Basis Set Ventures的创始人兰雪昭表示，尽管女性在技术上面临所有障碍，但许多人仍然被人工智能吸引，因为它有可能解决人类问题。Basis Set Ventures是一家全女性合作伙伴团队，投资于早期的人工智能初创企业。
卡罗琳·罗兹(Carolyn RODZ)就是其中之一，她在与人共同创立Hello Alice之前，曾在投资银行、零售和数字媒体工作过，这是一家利用机器学习为其他企业家提供建议的初创公司。她说：“你不仅是在编写代码，而且是在以一种非常人性化的方式为人类编写代码--我认为这非常适合女性看待商业的方式，坦率地说，我认为这一领域一些最强大的人是女性，正因为这个原因。”
丽莎·法尔宗(Lisa Falzone)说，她在本科学习历史，但被人工智能所吸引，希望它能帮助解决枪支暴力问题。她的公司雅典娜安全公司(Athena Security)是她在2018年与人共同创立的，该公司使用视频监控来检测公共场所的枪支。
2010年，当法尔宗与人共同创立了她的第一家公司--销售点软件初创公司时，作为一名女性创始人感觉要孤单得多。
“那时候，我没有任何女性创办人朋友。感觉它们几乎不存在，“她说。现在，她说，她认为科技行业对女性企业家的支持更加有力，包括更好的网络和资金：“情况已经发生了很大变化。”
不过，她表示，还有一段路要走。她指出，尽管她的高管团队大多是女性，但只有大约四分之一的开发人员是女性。
Diaspora AI的首席执行官兼创始人卡拉·弗莱明(Kala Fleming)表示，在许多公司设置中，她习惯了成为房间里唯一的女性或黑人。Diaspora AI为水利基础设施管理提供了一个人工智能平台。她说，这让她想要更努力地工作，因为她的目标是帮助解决水管理问题，特别是在发展中国家。
她说，人工智能仍然是一个较新的领域，这一事实让它感觉更平易近人：“这个空间有一些东西，即使是一个小家伙也可以开始真正重要的对话。”
陈女士是“华尔街日报”驻费城的记者。可以通过te-ping.chen@wsj.com联系到她。
出现在2020年9月30日印刷版上的“初创公司的女性创始人瞄准了人工智能偏见”。
note_en:
Journal Reports: Leadership     They see gender diversity in their companies as crucial in making sure the technology doesn’t penalize the underrepresented
   When Rana el Kaliouby was pitching investors in 2009 on her artificial-intelligence startup, Affectiva, she and her co-founder tried to steer clear of what she calls the “e-word”—emotion.
  They were both women, and though their startup was designed to detect emotion in technology, they were sensitive to how they would be perceived. They feared they might not be taken seriously because “emotion” wasn’t in the traditional lexicon of many companies and funders—and because it carried female connotations in a largely male industry.
  “We danced around it,” she says, adding that they called themselves a “sentiment” company instead. “Investors invest in what they know, and we were so different from what they were used to.”
  A decade later, that is changing. Though the field of artificial intelligence remains heavily male-dominated, female leaders have made noticeable inroads. Some say they want to build workplaces that are more inviting to diverse workforces, and help promote women in tech more broadly. Others say they want to try to address concerns with the technology, rooting out what they see as its potential biases against marginalized communities.
    “Technology won’t function well unless it is built by a diverse team,” says Dr. el Kaliouby, adding that the lack of gender diversity in Silicon Valley came as a contrast to what she had seen as a computer-science student in college in Egypt, where the gender breakdown was almost even. “My biggest concern in AI today isn’t that robots are going to take over, it’s that we’re unintentionally building bias into these systems.”
  Dr. el Kaliouby now embraces the word emotion in her description of her company, and in 2016 became the company’s CEO, after years of serving as its chief technology officer.
    The need to address biases in AI has lately been on stark display, with  Twitter saying it needed to do more analysis of its picture-cropping algorithm, after users found that it occasionally seemed to favor displaying white faces in tweet previews over Black ones. The company said it hadn’t found evidence of racial or gender bias in previous testing.
    In 2018, there were 3,477 venture-capital deals involving companies with at least one female founder, up from 583 in 2009, according to a report by  PitchBook and All Raise, a nonprofit that seeks to boost women in tech. And while 65% of large U.S. VC firms still don’t have a single female partner, a record 52 women were made partners last year, up from 38 the year prior, the group  found.
  Given that AI requires people to grapple with issues of fairness and representation in data, Kieran Snyder, CEO and co-founder of Textio, which uses artificial intelligence to analyze language in job descriptions, says it isn’t surprising that female founders and thought leaders have been especially visible in the field.
  Before starting her own company, Ms. Snyder worked at  Microsoft  where, she recalls, one incident in which her manager said she would have to wait for a promotion opportunity until after her maternity leave, when he would be able to better gauge whether she remained committed to her job. (Microsoft did not respond to a request for comment.)
  “I think people who’ve been outnumbered are more likely to have a sense of why an accurate, equitable data set is important, and to want to do that work,” she says, adding that women may be more drawn to the field as a consequence.
  The deficit of women in tech is in many ways a recent American phenomenon, says Lareina Yee, chief diversity and inclusion officer at McKinsey & Co. and a leader within the firm’s global tech practice. In 1995, for example, women accounted for 29% of all undergraduates studying computer sciences in the U.S. By two decades later, that figure had dropped to 18%, according to the National Science Foundation.
  And across Silicon Valley, while the percentage of women in technical roles has in some cases risen, it remains low: In 2019, for example, women accounted for 24% of technical U.S. roles at Google, up from 17% in 2014.
  Ms. Yee says one challenge is that Silicon Valley’s culture expects successful people to look like Mark Zuckerberg and has alienated those who don’t fit that mold. A new  report from Girls Who Code and Accenture, for example, found that half of women who take a job in tech quit the industry by age 35, with cultural issues ranking as the top reason.
  As a female CEO, Alice Zhang, co-founder of Verge Genomics, which uses AI to help identify new drugs, says she sets a different tone for the company. “There’s a lot of leadership styles where it’s all about pounding your fists on the table—‘We’re doing so well, we’re crushing it, we’re killing it,’ ” she says. “Mine is much more based around emotional vulnerability and honesty.”
  A study this month by global advisory company  Willis Towers Watson in collaboration with Bloomberg LP found that employees at companies with more gender balance in leadership tend to express higher engagement levels and a greater likelihood of staying with their employers.
    Before founding her company, Node, Falon Fatemi was one of Google’s early employees, where, she says, she was lucky to be mentored by women such as Stacy Brown-Philpot, her first manager and until recently CEO of TaskRabbit. Once she founded Node—which sells a software platform that helps businesses build and deploy AI models—Ms. Fatemi says she was deliberate about trying to create a female-friendly workplace, offering generous fertility benefits early on, and speaking frankly with co-workers about her own experience freezing her eggs.
  As a woman, Ms. Fatemi says certain things, like pitching to all-male rooms of investors, were harder. But others came more easily.
    “We always had diversity from day one—being a female founder, you attract that,” says Ms. Fatemi of her company, which has a roughly 40% female workforce, including product development and the leadership team.
  For all the obstacles women face in technology, many are still drawn to artificial intelligence for its potential to solve human problems, says Xuezhao Lan, founder of Basis Set Ventures, an all-female partner team that invests in early-stage AI startups.
  Among them is Carolyn Rodz, who worked in investment banking, retail and digital media before co-founding Hello Alice, a startup that uses machine learning to advise other entrepreneurs. “The idea that you’re not just building code, but you’re writing for humans in a very human way—I think it lends itself very well to the way women think about business, and frankly I think some of the strongest people in this field are women for that reason,” she says.
  Lisa Falzone says she studied history as an undergraduate, but was drawn to AI in hopes that it could help address gun violence. Her company, Athena Security, which she co-founded in 2018, uses video monitoring to detect guns in public places.
  In 2010, when Ms. Falzone co-founded her first company, a point-of-sale software startup, it felt much lonelier to be a female founder.
  “Back then, I didn’t have any women founder friends. It felt like they almost didn’t exist,” she says. Now, she says she sees more robust support for women entrepreneurs in tech, including better networks and funding: “The landscape has changed so much.”
  Still, there is a way to go, she says, noting that while her executive team is majority female, only around a quarter of her developers are women.
  Kala Fleming, CEO and founder of Diaspora AI, which offers an AI-powered platform for water-infrastructure management, says that in many corporate settings, she’s used to being the only woman or Black person in a room. She says that has made her want to work harder, fueled by her goal of helping solve water-management issues, particularly in the developing world.
  The fact that AI remains a newer field, she says, makes it feel more approachable: “There’s something about this space that allows even a little guy to start conversations that are really big.”
  Ms. Chen is a Wall Street Journal reporter in Philadelphia. She can be reached at  te-ping.chen@wsj.com.
  Appeared in the September 30, 2020, print edition as 'Female Founders Of Startups Take Aim at AI Bias.'
title: 人工智能初创公司的女性创始人谈到了科技领域的性别差距，她们如何试图建立多样化的工作场所，以及解决人工智能领域的潜在偏见
title_en: Women founders of AI startups talk about gender disparity in tech, how they are trying to build diverse workplaces, and addressing potential biases in AI

阿根廷漫画家华金·萨尔瓦多·拉瓦多(Joaquín萨尔瓦多Lavado)创造了马法尔达(Mafalda)这个人物，他在出生地门多萨去世，享年88岁。
动画片“玛法尔达”讲述的是一名6岁同名女孩的冒险故事，在西班牙语世界非常受欢迎。
拉瓦多在1964年至1973年间创作并绘制了这些漫画，但它们至今仍在重印。
马法尔达非常受欢迎，她甚至在阿根廷和西班牙都有自己的雕像。
这部漫画于1964年首次出现在阿根廷周刊Primera Plana上，讲述了玛法尔达(Mafalda)的日常生活，她是一对典型的阿根廷中产阶级夫妇的女儿，她经常用富有洞察力的问题让他们感到困惑。
马法尔达的机智和她对成人世界的敏锐观察确保了这部漫画的流行，这部漫画被翻译成了26种语言。
奎诺画了九年的漫画，直到1973年，他决定停止画漫画。几十年后，当被问及他的决定时，这位平面艺术家表示，他希望避免重复。
他说，这对许多艺术家来说都是一样的--例如，我已经受够了博特罗的胖乎乎的人，他指的是这位哥伦比亚画家，他的画以胖乎乎的动物和人物为特色。
他还说，拉丁美洲不断变化的政治格局影响了他停止绘制马法尔达的决定。
智利政变后，拉丁美洲的局势变得非常血腥，他谈到1973年邻国奥古斯特·皮诺切特将军将萨尔瓦多·阿连德赶下台。
如果我继续画她(马法尔达)，他们会开枪打我一到四次，他说，他指的是拉丁美洲反对右翼军事政权的艺术家和知识分子遭到的袭击。
基诺于1976年3月离开阿根廷前往意大利，几天前，军政府在他的祖国夺取了政权。在军事统治下，数以千计的政治对手被围捕并杀害。
1983年阿根廷恢复民主后，基诺在布宜诺斯艾利斯、马德里和米兰之间奔波。
note_en:
The Argentine cartoonist Joaquín Salvador Lavado, who created the character Mafalda, has died aged 88 in Mendoza, the city where he was born.
 Mafalda, the cartoon about the adventures of a six-year-old girl of the same name, is immensely popular in the Spanish-speaking world.
 Lavado wrote and drew the comics between 1964 and 1973 but they are still being reprinted to this day.
 Mafalda is so popular she even has her own statues in Argentina and in Spain.
 The comic, which first appeared in the Argentine weekly Primera Plana in 1964, features the daily life of Mafalda, the daughter of a typical middle-class Argentine couple, whom she often baffles with her insightful questions.
   Mafalda's wit and her sharp observations of the adult world ensured the comic's popularity, which was translated into 26 languages.
  Quino drew the comic strips for nine years until in 1973, he decided to stop. Asked about his decision, decades later, the graphic artist said he wanted to avoid repetition.
 "It's the same for many artists - for example, I've had enough of Botero's chubby people," he said referring to the Colombian painter whose paintings feature portly animals and characters.
 He also said that the changing political landscape in Latin America influenced his decision to stop drawing Mafalda.
 "After the coup d'etat in Chile, the situation in Latin America became very bloody," he said about the 1973 ousting from power of Salvador Allende by Gen August Pinochet in the neighbouring country.
 "If I had continued drawing her [Mafalda], they would have shot me once, or four times," he said referring to the attacks on artists and intellectuals who opposed right-wing military regimes in Latin America.
  Quino left Argentina for Italy in March 1976, days after a military junta had seized power in his native country. Thousands of political opponents were rounded up and killed under military rule.
 After democracy returned to Argentina in 1983, Quino split his time between Buenos Aires, Madrid and Milan.
title: 马法尔达漫画人物创作者基诺去世，享年88岁
title_en: Quino, creator of Mafalda comic character, dies aged 88

令人难以置信的故事，以智能家居软件被砍为开始，以资深议员否认接受超级大爆炸而结束。
特别报道俄亥俄州注册读者史蒂夫·林利喜欢他的电力公司的智能手机应用程序：它以15分钟的增量实时显示他的使用情况，并以简单易懂的图形显示他的费用。
“我并不总是使用它，”他告诉我们，“但如果我对我的使用有疑问，我可以回去看看发生了什么，并试图弄清楚当时我在做什么来增加我的账单。”这是一种人们发现非常有用的应用程序，对他们的生活和财务都有真正的影响。
因此，史蒂夫突然感到恼火，他的电力公司AEP俄亥俄州告诉他，在服务了三年后，这款应用将于本周停止运行。他要求解释，AEP告诉他，这是因为他们不再有监管部门的批准，“提供软件”。这说不通。
不过，令人难以置信的是，这是真的。但问题远不止于此。因为这是一个当智能家居和现代技术遇到愚蠢的官僚机构、根深蒂固的利益和现金捐赠时会发生什么的故事。
史蒂夫可能不知道的是，他的智能手机应用程序是2008年通过的一项法律的结果，该法律旨在应对气候变化，要求俄亥俄州到2027年从可再生能源获得12.5%的电力，同时通过能效计划将整体用电量减少22%。
这不是一个雄心勃勃的计划，但它是有意义的。然而，由于党派政治，自从这项法律在美国州通过以来，一群立法者-主要是共和党人，但也有一些民主党人-一直在试图扼杀这项法律。他们给出了各种各样的理由：这是政府的越权，它提高了电价，因为各种原因不再需要它，等等。
去年，经过11年的努力，他们终于如愿以偿了。众议院法案6于2019年4月以51票对38票通过，州长迈克·德文(R)于7月23日签署。
这项新法律实际上是为该州的发电厂运营商提供了13亿美元的救助。它取消了旨在应对气候变化的措施，这些措施让430万俄亥俄州家庭每月花费在3美元到4美元之间(取决于你问的是谁)，取而代之的是每月2.35美元的费用，这些费用将用于压倒性地补贴煤炭和核电站。
以只有真正失灵的政治才能做到的方式，新法律实际上并没有取消立法者最关注的事情-12.5%的可再生能源要求。这些授权仍然有效。取而代之的是，这项法律取消了通过能效计划减少22%的能源。
你可能已经猜到了，其中一个程序是智能手机应用程序，它可以让数百万俄亥俄州居民看到他们实际用的是什么电，花了多少钱，这样他们就能够就如何省钱做出明智的决定。
如果这一切听起来像是可怕的法律，那么情况会变得更糟：今年7月，在该法案通过一年多后，俄亥俄州众议院议长拉里·豪斯霍尔德(Larry Household)和其他四人因涉嫌收受6000万美元贿赂而被捕。
美国检察官大卫·德维莱尔(David Devillers)表示，这些指控纯粹是贿赂。这是交换条件。这是付费游戏。
根据美国联邦调查局(FBI)的说法，这项立法挽救了原定于2020年和2021年关闭的两座核电站，并根据新法律获得了数百万美元的重定向资金。联邦调查局追踪到大约6000万美元从核能巨头FirstEnergy及其附属公司流向一个名为“现在就生成”(Generation Now)的组织，该组织是一个501(C)(4)社会事业组织，并声称这8位数的资金中至少有50万美元最终进入了众议院议长豪斯霍尔德的银行账户。
联邦政府的调查仍在进行中。进一步的转折是，事实证明，就连AEP也向“现在的一代”(Generation Now)捐款。在过去的一个月里，俄亥俄州众议院的一个特别委员会就是否废除和取代这项处于核心地位的法律举行了三次听证会。
在贿赂指控浮出水面的前几个月，俄亥俄州公用事业委员会(Public Utilities Commission)在经历了一个有争议的公众意见过程[PDF]后，决定别无选择，只能终止因HB6而产生的能效要求，于9月30日结束这些要求，并于12月31日完全结束这些要求。
不只是智能手机应用程序会因为这次关闭和撤销监管审批而被淘汰：注定要失败的节能计划包括降低LED灯泡的价格(LED灯泡比传统白炽灯泡效率高得多，寿命也长得多，但购买成本更高)，以及智能恒温器、节能设备的回扣，以及让家庭变得更节能的各种经济激励措施-绝缘、回收旧家电等等。
实际上，支持低效灯泡和其他旧技术的努力削弱了现代技术，如应用程序和智能恒温器，尽管后者导致人们使用和支付更少的能源。
这款智能手机应用程序是由密歇根州一家名为Powerley的公司开发和运营的。当这款应用宣布时，其首席执行官马诺伊·库马尔(Manoj Kumar)兴奋地表示，它将成为“全球首批提供能源驱动的智能家居体验的公用事业公司之一”，通过“超越电表”让客户实时了解他们的能源使用情况。
这款应用程序是免费的，而且使用量很大。这项服务被命名为It‘s Your Power，Android和iOS应用程序都与一个名为能量桥(Energy Bridge)的集线器协同工作，该集线器还允许添加其他智能家居设备，如灯泡和传感器。
与苹果、亚马逊和谷歌越来越多地推动的系统不同，该系统旨在以一种对房主有用的方式添加新技术，而不会将数据泄露给大型科技巨头，这些巨头随后会围绕这些技术建立广告和销售系统。
It‘s Your Power网站现在已经死了，数百万不满的客户被告知，他们的应用程序从周四起将不再起作用，尽管史蒂夫后来告诉我们，截止日期已经延长到10月底。我们代表他询问了Powerley，他是否还有办法让这款应用继续运行，如果有，成本是多少：这家公司陷入了一场政治丑闻，只是想把一款应用交到人们手中，谨慎是可以理解的。
我会参考俄亥俄州AEP的项目网站，了解关于这款应用程序和可用性的最新信息，其营销总监马特·莫瓦特(Matt Mowat)告诉“登记册”(The Register)。我知道一旦有更多的信息可用，该网站将会更新。
本月早些时候，61岁的豪斯霍尔德在被捕后被剥夺了众议院议长的职务，他对联邦敲诈勒索指控提出无罪抗辩。
其他四名被美联储锁定的人-游说者尼尔·克拉克(Neil Clark)和胡安·塞斯佩德斯(Juan Cespedes)，俄亥俄州共和党前主席马特·博尔赫斯(Matt Borges)，以及政治策略师杰夫·朗斯特罗斯(Jeff Longstreth)-也提出无罪抗辩。®。
The Register-独立于科技界的新闻和观点。情况发布的一部分
note_en:
Incredible tale that starts with smart home software being axed and ends with senior lawmaker denying taking a super-bung
  Special report Ohio  Register reader Steve Ringley loves his electricity company’s smartphone app: it shows his usage in real time in 15-minute increments along with its cost in a simple and easy-to-understand graphic.
  “I do not use it all the time,” he told us, “but if I have questions about my usage I can go back and see what happened and try to figure out what I was doing to run my bill up at that time.” It’s the kind of app people find incredibly useful and has a real impact on their lives - and finances.
  So Steve was annoyed when out of the blue, his electricity company AEP Ohio told him after three years of service the app would stop working this week. He asked for an explanation, and AEP told him it was because they "no longer have regulatory approval,” to offer the software. Which makes no sense.
  Except, incredibly, it’s true. But the issue goes way deeper than that. Because this is a story of what happens when smart homes and modern technology meet dumb bureaucracies, entrenched interests, and cash donations.
  What Steve may not have known is that his smartphone app was the result of a law passed in 2008 aimed at tackling climate change by requiring Ohio to get 12.5 per cent of its power from renewable sources by 2027, while at the same time reducing overall power usage by 22 per cent through energy-efficiency programs.
  It wasn’t a massively ambitious program but it was something. Thanks to partisan politics, however, ever since the law was passed in the US state, a group of lawmakers – mostly Republican but some Democrats – have been trying to kill it. They have given a wide variety of reasons why: it is government overreach, it raises the price of electricity, it’s no longer needed for various reasons, and so on.
  Last year, after 11 years of trying, they finally got their way.  House Bill 6 passed by a 51-38 vote in April 2019, and governor Mike DeWine (R) signed it off on July 23.
  The new law was effectively a $1.3bn bailout for power plant operators in the state. It removed the measures designed to tackle climate change, which were costing 4.3 million Ohioan households between $3 and $4 each month (depending on who you ask), and replaced them with $2.35 in monthly charges that will be used to overwhelmingly subsidize coal and nuclear plants instead.
    In the way that only truly dysfunctional politics can, the new law didn’t actually remove the thing that lawmakers were most focused on – the 12.5 per cent renewable energy requirement. Those mandates stay. Instead, the law killed off the 22 per cent energy reduction through efficiency programs.
  And one of those programs, you may have guessed, was the smartphone app that allowed millions of Ohio residents to see what electricity they were actually using and how much it cost them so they were able to make smart decisions about how to save money.
    And if all that sounds like terrible law, it gets worse: this July, more than a year after the law passed, Ohio House Speaker Larry Householder (R) and four others were  arrested on suspicion of taking  a $60m bribe to pass the bill.
  "These allegations are bribery pure and simple," said US Attorney David DeVillers. "This was quid pro quo. This was pay to play."
  According to the FBI, two nuclear power plants that were due to close in 2020 and 2021 were saved by the legislation, and were given millions in redirected funds under the new law. The Feds tracked some $60m flowing from a nuclear energy giant FirstEnergy and its affiliates into an outfit called Generation Now, set up as a 501(c)(4) social cause organization, and claimed at least $500,000 of that eight-figure sum ended up in House speaker Householder's bank account.
  The federal government's investigation is still ongoing. In a further twist, it turns out even AEP  contributed money to Generation Now. And in the past month, an Ohio House select committee has held three hearings over whether to repeal and replace the law at the center of it all.
  Many months before the bribery allegations emerged, Ohio's Public Utilities Commission, following a  contentious public comment process [PDF], decided it had no choice but to end the energy-efficiency mandates as a result of HB6, winding them down on September 30, and ending them completely on December 31.
  It's not just smartphone apps that'll be unplugged as a consequence of this shut down and withdrawal of regulatory approval: the doomed  energy-efficiency programs included things like reducing the price of LED bulbs – which are much more efficient and last much longer than traditional incandescent bulbs but cost more at purchase – as well as rebates on smart thermostats, energy-saving devices and various financial incentives to make home more energy efficient – insulation, recycling old appliances, and the like.
    In effect, the drive to support inefficient bulbs and other old technology undercut modern tech, such as apps and smart thermostats, even though the latter results in people using and paying less for their energy.
  The smartphone app was developed and run by a company called Powerley, based in Michigan, and when it was announced, its CEO Manoj Kumar was excited that it AEP Ohio would be “one of the first utilities globally to deliver the energy-driven smart home experience,” by “going beyond the meter” to give customers a real-time insight into their energy usage.
  The app was free and take-up was huge. The service was branded  It’s Your Power and both Android and iOS apps were produced that worked with a hub, called the Energy Bridge, that also allowed for the addition of other smart home devices like bulbs and sensors.
  Unlike the systems being increasingly pushed by Apple, Amazon, and Google, the system was designed to add new technology in a way that is useful to homeowners without it leaking data to huge tech giants who then build advertising and sales systems around them.
  The  It’s Your Power website is now dead, and millions of unhappy customers were informed that their app will no longer work from Thursday, although Steve since tells us the cut-off has been  extended to the end of October. We asked Powerley on his behalf if there’s a way he can still keep the app working and if so how much it will cost: the company, stuck in the middle of a political scandal while simply trying to put an app in people's hands, was understandably cautious.
  "I would refer to AEP Ohio’s program website for the latest information on the app and availability," its marketing director Matt Mowat told  The Register. "I know the site will be updated once additional information becomes available."
  Earlier this month, Householder, 61, who was stripped of his House speaker role after his arrested,  pleaded not guilty to a federal racketeering charge.
  The other four collared by the Feds – lobbyists Neil Clark and Juan Cespedes, Ohio GOP ex-chairman Matt Borges, and political strategist Jeff Longstreth – have also pleaded not guilty. ®

有时候，打电话是把事情做好的最好方式。我们打电话给零售商来定位丢失的包裹，打电话给公用事业公司来调整我们的网速，打电话给航空公司来改变我们的旅行路线……清单不胜枚举。但通常情况下，我们需要在这些呼叫过程中等待等待-近距离收听音乐和重复的信息-然后才能联系到可以提供帮助的客户支持代表。事实上，美国人上周花了超过1000万个小时与企业保持联系。
Hold For Me，我们最新的手机应用程序功能，可以帮助你找回这段时间，从美国Pixel 5和Pixel 4a(5G)的早期预览开始。现在，当你拨打免费号码，有业务让你等待时，谷歌助手可以在线为你等待。你可以回到你的生活，一旦有人在线并准备通话，谷歌助手就会在你的屏幕上用声音、振动和提示通知你。这意味着你会花更多的时间做对你来说重要的事情，而不是花更少的时间听保持音乐。
note_en:
Sometimes, a phone call is the best way to get something done. We call retailers to locate missing packages, utilities to adjust our internet speeds, airlines to change our travel itineraries...the list goes on. But more often than not, we need to wait on hold during these calls—listening closely to hold music and repetitive messages—before we reach a customer support representative who can help. In fact, people in the United States spent over 10 million hours on hold with businesses last week.
  Hold for Me, our latest  Phone app feature, helps you get that time back, starting with an early preview on Pixel 5 and Pixel 4a (5G) in the U.S. Now, when you call a toll-free number and a business puts you on hold, Google Assistant can wait on the line for you. You can go back to your day, and Google Assistant will notify you with sound, vibration and a prompt on your screen once someone is on the line and ready to talk. That means you’ll spend more time doing what’s important to you, and less time listening to hold music.
